{{short description|Type of conflict}}

{{Psychology sidebar}}
'''Work–family conflict''' is a concept in [[psychology]], [[industrial and organizational psychology]], and [[occupational health psychology]] that studies how competing demands between work and family responsibilities create tension and measurably impact mental health, productivity, and family dynamics.<ref>Greenhaus, J. H., & Beutell, N. J. (1985). Sources and conflict between work and family roles. Academy of Management Review, 10(1), 76-88.</ref> The concept emerged as industrialization shifted paid work out of the household, reshaping expectations at the ''work-life interface''.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014">{{Cite journal|last=Lavassani & Movahedi|first=Kayvan Miri & Bahar|date=2014|title=DEVELOPMENTS IN THEORIES AND MEASURES OF WORK-FAMILY RELATIONSHIPS: FROM CONFLICT TO BALANCE|url=http://davidwimer.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/03/kayvan-miri-lavassani-bahar-movahedi-work-family-life-6-19.pdf|journal=Contemporary Research on Organization Management and Administration|volume=2|pages=2335–7959|archive-date=2018-07-12|access-date=2018-05-01|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180712142955/http://davidwimer.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/03/kayvan-miri-lavassani-bahar-movahedi-work-family-life-6-19.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> Work–family conflict has been associated with increased [[occupational burnout]], [[job stress]], poorer health, and diminished [[organizational commitment]] and [[job performance]].<ref>Amstad, F. T., Meier, L. L., Fasel, U., Elfering, A., & Semmer, N. K. (2011). A meta-analysis of work–family conflict and various outcomes with a special emphasis on cross-domain versus matching-domain relations. Journal of Occupational Health Psychology, 16(2), 151-169. {{doi|10.1037/a0022170}}</ref>

==Origins and theory==
Scholars began examining work–family conflict in the late 19th century as industrialization moved income-generating labor from the home to factories, altering the relationship between work and family life.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" />
Boundary theory frames social life as two interdependent domains, work and family, with distinct roles and responsibilities.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" /> Because the domains influence one another, individuals must transition between expectations at work and roles within the family.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" />
Border theory builds on boundary theory by considering how each domain influences the other and by identifying strategies people use to manage the borders between them.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" /> Some individuals separate the domains and move back and forth between them, while others integrate them to pursue balance.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" />

===Forms of conflict===
[[File:Work-Family Spillover.png|thumb|alt=Bi-directional relationship of Work-to-Family and Family-to-Work|Adopted from Lavassani & Movahedi (2014), ''Work–life interface'']]
Work–family conflict runs in two directions: ''work-to-family conflict'' and ''family-to-work conflict''.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" /> Work-to-family conflict arises when job demands such as long or inflexible hours, heavy workloads, interpersonal friction, travel, or unsupportive supervision disrupt responsibilities at home, for example causing a parent to miss after-school pick-up.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" /> Family-to-work conflict occurs when caregiving duties, family strain, or limited support at home interfere with job performance, including situations where employees must leave work to care for a sick relative.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" /> Each direction can be ''time-based'' when schedules collide, ''strain-based'' when stress spills over, or ''behavior-based'' when the conduct required in one role clashes with the other.<ref>Greenhaus, J. H., & Powell, G. N. (2006). When work and family are allies: A theory of work-family enrichment. Academy of Management Review, 25, 178–199.</ref>

Although work interface with family (WIF) and family interface with work (FIW) are strongly correlated, more attention has been directed toward WIF. Research linked to Arlie Russell Hochschild's concept of the "ideal worker" depicts the inelastic nature of work roles and responsibilities.<ref name="Hochschild-1997">Hochschild, Arlie Russell. 1997. The Time Bind: When Work Becomes Home and Home Becomes Work. New York: Metropolitan Books.</ref> The expectations employers hold of an ideal worker assume that family responsibilities are handled elsewhere, leaving employees unencumbered at work.<ref name="Hochschild-1997" /> Although most families in the United States are dual earning, the image of the ideal worker persists and reinforces work–family conflict.<ref name="Hochschild-1997" />

===Workaholism===
Workaholism correlates strongly with work-to-family conflict because prioritizing work commitments crowds out family time.<ref name="Lavassani & Movahedi-2014" /> Scholars describe workaholism as a substantial investment in work that can manifest in multiple directions and facets.<ref name="Harpaz-2003">{{Cite journal|title=Workaholism: Its Definition and Nature|journal = Human Relations|volume = 56|issue = 3|pages = 291–319|last=Harpaz|first=Itzhak|date=March 2003|doi = 10.1177/0018726703056003613| s2cid=145409705 }}</ref> Job flexibility and satisfaction influence how strain transfers between work and home, while excessive work hours often displace intimate relationships and social connections.<ref name="Harpaz-2003" /> Workaholics devote extensive time to work-related activities and may sacrifice family, social, and recreational life, contributing to marital strain and isolation.<ref name="Harpaz-2003" />

Employers have become more aware of these pressures and have implemented family-responsive human resource practices that promote work-family balance to reduce stress in both environments.<ref name="Harpaz-2003" />

==Organizational responses==
Researchers compare high-pressure corporate roles to elite athletics, noting that sustained exertion without recovery produces [[chronic stress]], burnout, and fatigue.<ref name="Harvard Business Review-2001">{{Cite news|url=https://hbr.org/2001/01/the-making-of-a-corporate-athlete|title=The Making of a Corporate Athlete|date=2001-01-01|work=Harvard Business Review|access-date=2018-03-20}}</ref> Employers counter these risks by encouraging short recovery breaks and providing facilities such as corporate gyms, which can improve cognition, energy, focus, and [[emotional intelligence]].<ref name="Harvard Business Review-2001" />

Organizations seek an advantage by branding themselves as [[work-life balance]] supportive, a stance linked to higher retention, morale, performance, and organizational identification.<ref name="Mescher-2010">{{Cite journal|last1=Mescher|first1=Samula|last2=Benschop|first2=Yvonne|last3=Doorewaard|first3=Hans|title=Representations of work-life balance support|journal=Human Relations|volume=63|issue=1|pages=21–39|doi=10.1177/0018726709349197|year=2010|s2cid=146224876 |hdl=2066/87048|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Family-friendly policies covering paid parental leave, sick leave, health insurance, and subsidized or on-site child care are associated with lower work-family conflict.<ref name="Waldfogel, J. 2001">Waldfogel, J. (2001). International Policies Toward Parental Leave and Child Care. Caring for Infants and Toddlers, 99–110.</ref>

Advances in communication technology allow employees on tight schedules to stay connected with family members, and organizations increasingly use [[remote work]] programs and expanded [[flextime]] to provide that flexibility.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Temple|first=H.|last2=Gillespie|first2=B.|date=February 2009|title=Taking charge of work and life|journal=ABA Journal|volume=95|pages=31–32}}</ref><ref>Pitt-Catsouphes, Marcie; Casy, Judi; Shulkin, Sandee; Weber, Julie; Curlew, Mary. (2009). Telework and Telecommuting: Policy Briefing Series. Boston: Sloan Work and Family Research Network. [https://workfamily.sas.upenn.edu/sites/workfamily.sas.upenn.edu/files/imported/pdfs/policy_makers3.pdf]</ref><ref>Estes, S. B., & Glass, J. L. (1997). The Family Responsive Workplace. Annual Review of Sociology, 289–310.</ref>

==Role of gender==
Gendered expectations, especially the "ideal worker" norm that prizes constant availability, shape how organizations evaluate employees with caregiving duties.<ref name="King, E. 20082">King, E. (2008). The effect of bias and unrealistic on the advancement of working mothers: Disentangling legitimate concerns from inaccurate stereotypes as predictors of advancement in academe. Human Relations, 61, 1677–1711. {{doi|10.1177/0018726708098082}}</ref> Managers who subscribe to this standard often read visible work-family juggling as lack of commitment, which reduces promotion prospects.<ref name="Hoobler-2009">{{Cite journal|last1=Hoobler|first1=J.|last2=Wayne|first2=S.|last3=Lemmon|first3=G.|date=2009|title=Bosses' perception of family-work conflict and women's promotability: Glad ceiling effects|journal=Academy of Management Journal|volume=52|issue=5|pages=939–957}}</ref>

Women experience that bias acutely because they continue to shoulder substantial unpaid care work. German dual-earner households with children under thirteen reported that mothers provided nearly triple the child care of fathers in 2018, while studies find women have less control over their schedules, heightening work–family conflict.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Arntz |first=Melanie |last2=Ben Yahmed |first2=Sarra |last3=Berlingieri |first3=Francesco |date=November 2020 |title=Working from Home and COVID-19: The Chances and Risks for Gender Gaps |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s10272-020-0938-5 |journal=Intereconomics |language=en |volume=55 |issue=6 |pages=381–386 |doi=10.1007/s10272-020-0938-5 |issn=0020-5346 |pmc=7704591 |pmid=33281218}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title=In search of family-friendly careers? Professional strategies, work conditions and gender differences in work–family conflict|journal = Community, Work & Family|volume = 21|last1=Grönlund|first1=Anne|last2=Öun|first2=Ida|year=2017|pages=87–105|doi=10.1080/13668803.2017.1375460|doi-access=free}}</ref> Managers may therefore rate women who balance care and employment as less committed, while men reporting lower conflict are seen as especially dedicated.<ref name="Cinnamon Gali-2002">{{cite journal|last1=Cinnamon Gali|first1=Rachel|last2=Rich|first2=Yisrael|date=December 2002|title=Gender Differences in the Importance of Work and Family Roles: Implications for Work–Family Conflict|journal=Sex Roles|volume=47|issue=11–12|pages=531–541|doi=10.1023/A:1022021804846|s2cid=140364709 }}</ref><ref name="King, E. 20082" /><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Ferguson|first1=Merideth|last2=Carlson|first2=Dawn|last3=Hunter|first3=Emily|last4=Whitten|first4=Dwayne|date=October 2012|title=A two-study examination of work–family conflict, production deviance and gender|journal=Journal of Vocational Behavior|volume=81|issue=2|pages=245–258|doi=10.1016/j.jvb.2012.07.004|s2cid=143684698 }}</ref>

Studies of fathers find that those with limited caregiving experience often underestimate household demands, whereas men whose spouses take on most domestic labor remain more visible at work and frequently shape workplace policy.<ref name="Huffman-2014">{{cite journal|title=Gender role beliefs and fathers' work-family conflict|last1=Huffman|first1=Ann H.|last2=Olson|first2=Kristine J.|journal=Journal of Managerial Psychology|volume=29|issue=7|date=September 2014|publisher=Emerald Publishing|doi=10.1108/JMP-11-2012-0372|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/265969317_Gender_role_beliefs_and_fathers'_work-family_conflict}}</ref><ref name="Williams, J. 20002">Williams, J. (2000). Unbending gender: Why family and work conflict and what to do about it. New York, NY: Oxford University Press</ref>

The [[motherhood penalty]] captures the cumulative outcome: working mothers face documented gaps in pay, perceived competence, and access to benefits and continue to receive lower performance evaluations than non-mothers, reinforcing discrimination in pay, hiring, and daily treatment.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Anderson|first=Deborah|author2=Melissa Binder|author3=Kate Krause|year=2003|title=The Motherhood Wage Penalty Revisited: Experience, Heterogeneity, Work effort and Work-Schedule Flexibility|journal=Industrial and Labor Relations Review|volume=56|issue=2|pages=273–294|doi=10.2307/3590938|jstor=3590938}}</ref><ref name="Paik">{{cite journal|last=Correll|first=S.|author2=S. Bernard|author3=I. Paik|year=2007|title=Getting a job: Is there a motherhood penalty?|journal=American Journal of Sociology|volume=112|issue=5|pages=1297–1338|doi=10.1086/511799|citeseerx=10.1.1.709.8363|s2cid=7816230 }}</ref><ref name="BudigEngland">{{cite journal|last=Budig|first=Michelle|author2=Paula England|year=2001|title=The Wage Penalty for Motherhood|journal=American Sociological Review|volume=66|issue=2|pages=204|doi=10.2307/2657415|jstor=2657415|citeseerx=10.1.1.512.8060}}</ref><ref name="Williams, J. 20002" />

==Sources==
* Bakker, A., Demerouti, E. & Burke, R. (January 2009). Workaholism and Relationship Quality: A Spillover-Crossover Perspective. ''Journal of Occupational Health Psychology, 14'', 23–33
* Frone, M. R., Yardley, J. K., & Markel, K. S. (1997). Developing and testing an integrative model of the work–family interface. ''Journal of Vocational Behavior, 50'', 145–167.
* Greenhaus, J. H., & Beutell, N. J. (1985). Sources of conflict between work and family roles. ''Academy of Management Review, 10'', 76–88.
* Kossek, E. E., & Ozeki, C. (1998). Work–family conflict, policies, and the job–life satisfaction relationship: A review and directions for organizational behavior–human resources research. ''Journal of Applied Psychology, 83'', 139–149.
* Kossek, E., Noe, R. & DeMarr, B. (April 1999). Work-family synthesis: Individual and organizational determinants.''International Journal of Conflict Management, 10'', 102–129.
* Krouse, S. S., & Afifi, T. D. (2007). Family-to-work spillover stress: Coping communicatively in the workplace. ''The Journal of Family Communication, 7'', 85–122.
* Lambert, S. J. (1990). Processes linking work and family: A critical review and research agenda. ''Human Relations, 43'', 239–257.
* MacDermind, S. M., Seery, B. L., & Weiss, H. H. (2002). An emotional examination of the work-family interface. In N. Schmitt (Series Ed.) & R. G. Lord, R. J. Klimoski & R. K. Kanfer (Vol. Eds.), ''The organizational frontier series: Vol. 16. Emotions in the workplace: Understanding the structure and role of emotions in organizational behavior'' (pp.&nbsp;402–427). San Francisco: Jossey-Bass.
* Temple, H. & Gillespie, B. (February 2009). Taking charge of work and life. ''ABA Journal, 95'', 31–32.
